title: Thrown stoneware bowl
subtitle: A wheel-thrown open bowl in smooth stoneware, trimmed and glaze-fired at cone 6
excerpt: A wheel-thrown bowl in smooth stoneware: centred, opened, pulled to a curved wall, trimmed at leather-hard, bisque-fired at cone 06, glazed by dipping, and glaze-fired to cone 6 (1222°C). Beginner-appropriate size: 500 g of clay producing a bowl roughly 140 mm across and 70 mm deep.
sourceNotes: Thrown bowl construction derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: Pushing the clay mound into a state where it rotates without wobbling; the essential first step before opening.
Drawing clay upward from the floor of an opened mound to form the bowl wall, using coordinated inside and outside hand pressure.
A recessed ring cut into the base of the leather-hard bowl that lifts it off the kiln shelf and refines the base profile.
The first firing at cone 06 (999°C) that converts greenware into durable, porous bisqueware ready for glazing.

aliases: how to throw a bowl on the wheel

projectSchedule.title: Throw and wire off
projectSchedule.body: Centre, open, pull, shape. Wire off and leave on board to firm.

projectSchedule.title: Trim the foot ring
projectSchedule.body: At leather-hard (8-24 hours later). Trim foot ring with loop tool.

projectSchedule.title: Bisque fire
projectSchedule.body: Fully bone-dry before loading. Cone 06 (999°C). Cool before unloading.

projectSchedule.title: Glaze and glaze fire
projectSchedule.body: Wax foot ring, dip in glaze, load kiln, fire to cone 6 (1222°C).

attrs.title: Kiln safety
attrs.body: Never open the kiln above 90°C. Glaze contains metal oxides; do not use the finished bowl for food or drink unless the glaze is rated food-safe and fired to the manufacturer's specified cone. Ventilate the kiln area during both bisque and glaze firings.

content.text: A simple thrown bowl is the most direct project for learning how centring, opening, and pulling work together. Five hundred grams of smooth stoneware produces a bowl roughly 140 mm across and 70 mm deep, large enough to use as a cereal bowl. The project takes four separate sessions across two to three weeks: throwing, trimming, bisque, and glaze firing.

items.name: Pre-mixed cone 6 glaze
items.quantity: enough to dip a 140 mm bowl

content.text: Throwing the bowl
1. Centre 500 g
Wedge and slap the clay onto the wheel head.
Centring
is complete when the clay feels still under resting palms: no pulsing, no vibration. Take the time to centre fully before opening.
2. Open to a wide, shallow floor

Open the mound wider and shallower than for a cylinder; a bowl shape needs more floor width and less height. Press thumbs down 8-10 mm from the wheel head and pull outward to a floor diameter of 80-90 mm. Compress the floor with a circular sweep.
3. Pull and flare the wall
Pull

the wall upward and slightly outward with each pass. After the first pull produces a low cylinder, use a wooden rib against the outer wall while the inside hand presses gently outward to open the bowl curve. The rim should flare out at a wider angle than the base. Aim for walls 6-8 mm thick throughout.
Once shaped, smooth the rim with a damp chamois. Wire off the base and leave on the bat or wheel head to firm to leather-hard.
Trimming the foot ring
When leather-hard (8-24 hours), mount the bowl upside-down on the wheel head, centred. Mark the
foot ring
diameter and cut with a loop tool. Remove excess base clay to lighten the bowl and establish a clean foot ring. The foot ring should sit about 15-20 mm inside the outer wall line.
Drying and bisque firing
Dry slowly under light plastic for the first 24 hours, then open. Full drying to bone-dry takes two to five days depending on wall thickness and room humidity. Load when fully dry and
bisque fire
to cone 06 (999°C) using a slow climb schedule.
Glazing and glaze firing

Wax the foot ring. Dip the bowl in a bucket of cone 6 glaze, holding by the foot ring, submerging fully, and lifting out with a slow rotation. Allow to dry before loading. Fire to cone 6 (1222°C) following the manufacturer's schedule.

items.symptom: Bowl warps during drying; rim goes oval
items.cause: One side of the rim dried faster than the other, or rim too thin
items.fix: Cover with plastic for slower, even drying; compress the rim with a damp chamois immediately after throwing

items.symptom: Base too thick after trimming; heavy and chunky
items.cause: Floor was not opened wide enough, or too much clay used for the bowl size
items.fix: Trim more aggressively; the recessed base area can be trimmed to 8-10 mm without risk, use a knuckle tap test to check

items.symptom: Glaze crawls off in patches during firing
items.cause: Glaze applied too thick, or dust and oils on bisqueware surface before glazing
items.fix: Wipe bisqueware with a damp sponge before dipping; dip at a consistent speed for even coat thickness
